title: The Scream
medium: Oil, tempera, pastel and crayon on cardboard
description: The most famous image of existential dread in all of art, The Scream depicts a figure on a bridge clutching its face as the sky behind erupts in swirling bands of blood-red and sickly orange. Munch described the inspiration: 'I felt a great, unending scream piercing through nature.' The painting's power comes from its radical simplification — the undulating lines of sky, water, and landscape merge into a single visual shriek.

title: Madonna
description: Munch's Madonna is no sacred icon but a sensuous woman caught in a moment of ecstatic abandon, her dark hair flowing around her naked body in sinuous Art Nouveau curves. The blood-red halo and swirling dark background transform the subject into a primal vision of desire, conception, and mortality. The lithographic border of the later prints added sperm and a fetal figure, making the life-death cycle explicit.

title: The Dance of Life
description: Three versions of the same woman — in white innocence, red passion, and black mourning — frame this allegorical dance on a midsummer night by the fjord. Couples swirl across the moonlit grass in a primal ritual, while the column of moonlight on the water pulses like a living thing. Munch distills the entire arc of romantic love into a single frozen moment.

title: Street, Berlin
location: Museum of Modern Art, New York
description: Kirchner's jagged, angular vision of Berlin street life captures the nerve-jangling energy of the modern city. Elegantly dressed women — their features reduced to sharp, mask-like planes — stride through a compressed urban space rendered in clashing pinks, blacks, and acid greens. The painting vibrates with metropolitan anxiety and sexual tension, its fractured forms owing as much to African sculpture as to Cubism.

title: The Large Blue Horses
description: Three monumental horses curve together in a rhythmic arc, their bodies rendered in a deep, saturated blue that Marc associated with spirituality and masculinity. The rolling hills behind them pulse with complementary yellows, reds, and greens, creating a landscape of pure emotional color. Marc believed animals possessed an innocence that granted them a deeper connection to the spiritual essence of nature than humans could achieve.

title: Composition VII
description: Kandinsky's monumental Composition VII is widely considered his masterpiece and one of the first truly abstract paintings. A maelstrom of color and form erupts across the enormous canvas — swirling lines, radiating circles, and jagged shards of vivid pigment collide in a visual symphony that the artist compared to a flood, a resurrection, and the Last Judgment simultaneously. Kandinsky completed the final canvas in just four days after months of preparatory studies.

title: The Embrace
description: Two intertwined figures cling to each other on crumpled white sheets, their bodies rendered with Schiele's characteristic angular intensity and raw, exposed vulnerability. The tangled limbs and grasping hands convey desperate intimacy, while the rumpled fabric surrounding them creates a nest-like cocoon. This late work shows Schiele's line becoming more fluid and his palette warmer, even as the emotional intensity remains undiminished.
